narrative: As a white male applicant with an upper-middle-class family background, your law school application was evaluated using a model trained on data from thousands of students who took the bar exam, predicting their likelihood of passing on the first try. The model assessed your profile— including your undergraduate GPA of 3.10, LSAT score of 37, full-time student status, and school decile rankings from Years 1 and 3—and calculated a 91.9% probability that you would fail the bar exam. This high predicted risk of failure is why your application was rejected, as the school prioritizes admitting students likely to succeed on the bar from the outset. The decision hinged most heavily on your Year 3 school decile ranking of 1, which placed you in the bottom tenth of your peers and strongly pushed the prediction toward failure, signaling to the model a significant drop or struggle in performance later in your studies. Next in influence was your Year 1 decile ranking, also a 1 compared to the typical student's midpoint around 5 or 6, further reinforcing the model's concern about consistently low academic standing relative to others. Your LSAT score of 37, slightly above the average of 36.7, worked in your favor by nudging the prediction somewhat toward passing, showing strength in that area amid broader weaknesses. While your undergraduate GPA of 3.10 was a bit below the average 3.21 and your full-time status aligns with most applicants, these played lesser roles compared to the dominant impact of those early and later decile rankings. In essence, the model saw your position at the bottom of your school's grade distributions as the biggest red flags, outweighing positives like your LSAT and setting you apart from typical students who rank higher in deciles and show steadier academic trajectories. This isn't a judgment on your potential or background, but a data-driven flag on bar passage odds. To move forward, consider retaking courses or programs to boost your decile rankings, perhaps through community college or targeted prep that demonstrates upward trends, and reapply after building a stronger academic record. You might also explore part-time options or gap-year experiences to address these gaps, as many students in similar situations have turned things around with focused improvement. Your LSAT edge is a solid foundation—lean into that while tackling the areas that mattered most here. With persistence, you can shift the narrative for your next application.
